coredata.dc:description: The escalating environmental contamination with mercury has become a pressing issue, significantly impacting human beings and nature. For instance, small-scale gold mining has led to severe contamination in the Brazilian Yanomami village, highlighting the urgent need for action. The development of fibrillar-based sensors for mercury (II) ions represents an important issue to be considered in the point-of-care and simple detection of contaminants in water. Herein, this review discussed different colorimetric/photoluminescent-based prototypes for Hg2+ ions sensors and corresponding strategies to improve selectivity and sensitivity associated with the regeneration and reuse of the devices. Given these aspects, the electrospinning technique is promising for developing advanced mercury (II) ion sensors.

Reprinted from Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical, 215, Hongming Chen, Weihua Hu, Chang Ming Li, Colorimetric detection of mercury(II) based on 2,2′-bipyridyl induced quasi-linear aggregation of gold nanoparticles, Pages 421–427, Copyright (2015), with permission from Elsevier.

Description of the precursors and parameters applied in synthesizing functionalized carbon dots, characterized by fluorescence and the general suppression mechanism in the emission under interaction with Hg2+ ions. Reproduced from Ref. [50] with permission from the Royal Society of Chemistry.

Reprinted with permission from Ref. [70]. Copyright 2006 American Chemical Society.

head.ce:title: Recent advances in colorimetric and photoluminescent fibrillar devices, photonic crystals and carbon dot-based sensors for mercury (II) ion detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The escalating environmental contamination with mercury has become a pressing issue, significantly impacting human beings and nature. For instance, small-scale gold mining has led to severe contamination in the Brazilian Yanomami village, highlighting the urgent need for action. The development of fibrillar-based sensors for mercury (II) ions represents an important issue to be considered in the point-of-care and simple detection of contaminants in water. Herein, this review discussed different colorimetric/photoluminescent-based prototypes for Hg2+ ions sensors and corresponding strategies to improve selectivity and sensitivity associated with the regeneration and reuse of the devices. Given these aspects, the electrospinning technique is promising for developing advanced mercury (II) ion sensors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury is a pervasive and highly toxic environmental pollutant resulting from human activities (manufacturing, agriculture, and medicine-related production). Its widespread presence poses significant risks to wildlife and human health due to its bioaccumulation in soil, water, plants, and animals, underscoring the crucial role of the scientific community in addressing these risks. Mercury detection has proven significant advancements in recent years from the incorporation of nanotechnology-based systems for detection [1–6].

There are three different forms of mercury in nature: elemental mercury (Hg0), inorganic mercury (Hg+2), and the organic form (methylmercury). The elemental form of mercury is released in the atmosphere, oxidized to the inorganic form, and captured in the soil and water. Consequently, under the action of microorganisms, the biomethylation converts Hg2+ ions into methylmercury [7]. In particular, the organic form has been considered the most toxic form of mercury to living systems due to the ability of methylmercury to cross biological membranes [7]. The consumption of fish represents one of the most common ways to promote the entrance of organic mercury into the body [8,9]. Bioaccumulation of mercury results in several diseases, affecting the immune system, kidneys, and liver [10], being associated with neurodegenerative events such as Parkinson's and Alzheimer's diseases [11], with sequels of toxicity to fetal and infant [12]. This bioaccumulation underscores the severe risks of mercury pollution and the critical need for effective detection methods [13].

Therefore, it is imperative to rigorously monitor the presence of mercury in the air, water, soil, and products. The World Health Organization (WHO) has set the maximum acceptable level of Hg2+ ions in drinking water at 6 ppb [14–16], a limit that the US-EPA has further defined as 2 μg/L [17] while in China, this limit is 1 μg/L [18]. These stringent limits underscore the urgent need for research in the monitoring and developing sensors capable of adsorbing mercury-based species at ultra-low concentrations. With this aim, many approaches have been considered in the literature, including atomic absorption/emission spectroscopy [19], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [20,21], selective cold vapor atomic fluorescent spectrometry, and liquid chromatography (LC) [22–26].

Despite considering the relevance of these techniques for detecting mercury (II), there are limitations to be cited, such as expensive and time-consuming instrumentation. Consequently, more suitable methods for real-time and point-of-care monitoring are required. With this aim, colorimetric and fluorescent methods, with their low cost of production and instrumentation for detection, have been gaining attention. These methods could potentially allow for detection by the naked eye, representing a significant advancement in mercury detection [16,27]. The design of probe molecules for fluorescent detection of Hg2+ explores different strategies involving chelation-induced fluorescence changes and Hg2+- triggered specific reactions on the probe [23].

As a result, the fluorescence-based detection of mercury (II) uses the turn-on and turn-off processes relative to the emission of probe molecules, with the turn-off being the most common, divided into two classes: static quenching and dynamic quenching. Static quenching takes place at a condition in which the fluorophore and a target element (Hg2+) form a non-fluorescent complex, avoiding emission events. On the other hand, dynamic quenching is observed when the quencher deactivates the fluorophore to the ground state. The typical quenching is observed due to the collision between the fluorophore and Hg2+ ions, resulting in the electron transference to the conduction band, avoiding the electron/hole combination [28]. Strategies involving DNA-based probes explore the strong coordination of thymine and Hg2+ ions (due to the formation of complexes T-Hg2+-T) [29]. Given this mechanism, photonic crystals-based structures have been functionalized with T-rich ss DNA sensors, providing quenching in the fluorescence under conversion to T-Hg2+-T complexes.

In general, turn-off and turn-on processes are mutually explored, with the turn-on processes introducing advantages for detection due to the minimal influence of false negatives associated with an enhancement in the fluorescent under analytes detection. The most relevant methods for the optical spectroscopic detection of Hg2+ ions are schematically drawn in Fig. 1 , described as follows: the fluorescent-based methods (turn-on and turn-off methods) explore two types of quenching (static and dynamic) in which the fluorescence of sensing species is suppressed by Hg bonding (represented by electrospun fibers loaded with fluorescent nanoparticles that interact with mercury (II) ions losing their characteristic fluorescence). In the opposite direction, rhodamine derivatives and metal nanoparticles are excellent prototypes for the turn-on detection of Hg2+ ions by switching their behavior under bonding/releasing conditions in solution [30]. The quantum yield of rhodamine as a free molecule in bulk solution against the suppressed emission under interaction with auxiliary probe molecules (or metal nanoparticles) favors the sensitivity of the devices. Mutually explored in emissive and colorimetric sensors, metal nanoparticles (AuNPs and AgNPs) can suppress the surface plasmon resonance band (SPR) under the formation of amalgam compounds (Ag–Hg and Au–Hg), characterizing an important source for the development of colorimetric sensors, that are also favored by the aggregation of quantum carbon dots.

Herein, the most relevant optical-based methods for mercury (II) ion detection integrated into fibrillar devices (optical fibers and electrospun fibers), photonic crystals and carbon dots-based systems are discussed, given the new strategies applied in the increase of the sensitivity and selectivity associated with the removal of contaminants from the medium. With this aim, the chemical modification in conventional optical fibers, electrospun fibers, and photonic crystals represent promising strategies to push the state-of-art in chemosensors forward to reach values of the limit of detection in the order of a few ppt.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectrophotometric methods for Hg2+ ions detection

ce:section.ce:para: Colorimetric sensors explore the shift in the characteristic peak of catalysts to detect heavy metal ions with the metal nanoparticles being considered relevant prototypes of catalysts for the detection of Hg2+ ions. With this aim, the surface plasmon resonance band (SPR) shifts for gold and silver nanoparticles under aggregation, introducing an important role in the detection limit. The control in the color change is observed under aggregation/inhibition of aggregate formation induced by additives that switch the interaction between metal nanoparticles and Hg2+ ions [31–34].

Some examples of these compounds are the 2-2′-bipyridyl molecules that induce aggregation of AuNPs [31] and cyanuric acid (CA), characterized as an anti-aggregating component for AuNPs [35]. Under the presence of Hg2+ ions, CA-Hg2+-CA complexes are formed, and aggregation of AuNPs is favored, changing the solution's color from red to blue. Corresponding experiments were also conducted with silver nanoparticles [36].

The illustration for the resulting color change under the detection of Hg2+ is shown in Fig. 2 . As schematically drawn, 2-2′-bipyridyl molecules interact with AuNPs, leading to a quasi-linear aggregation of nanoparticles and following a change in color from red to blue under aggregation. Under the presence of mercury (II) ions, the aggregation process is inhibited, and the color of the solution remains red. At naked eye conditions, it is possible to detect the color as a characteristic response of SPR of isolated nanoparticles in the presence of an analyte under interest [31].

In general, surface plasmon polaritons optical sensors use the adsorptive properties of metal nanoparticle aggregates (such as gold and silver nanoparticles) to interact with the mercury vapor, affecting the plasmon band of the resulting compound [37]. Optoplasmonic sensors can combine optical nano/microcavities and plasmonic nanostructures for the sensing of single molecules – pushing forward the current state-of-the-art [38]. Borodaenko et al. [39] reported using laser-printed surface photoluminescence sensors enriched by surface plasmon from Ag nanoparticles incorporated into a fluidic device to detect the subnanomolar concentration of Hg2+ in water [39] by changes in the resulting fluorescence.

As an alternative to the SPR-based shifts, colorimetric sensors using N-doped carbon dots paired in complexes with cysteine have been explored [40]. Under the presence of Hg2+ ions, a complex of mercury (II) ions and cysteine is favored, resulting in the oxidation of carbon dots. Cysteine can also be combined with chitosan/Cu/Fe composites to inhibit the oxidation of 3,3′5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine, which favors the rapid colorimetric detection of Hg2+. [41]. Another photoluminescent complex applied in colorimetric sensors is the fluorescein/thiourea conjugated complex. The formation of complex thiourea-Hg2+ results in the protonation of the fluorescein dye changing to a red color in the resulting solution [42].

Ibarlucea et al. [43] reported an alternative strategy, expanding the concept of lab-on-a-chip from electrochemistry to a photonic lab-on-a-chip in which the colorimetric detection of Hg2+ occurs. The detection process is based on the action of fluorescent ligands, which form stable complexes that produce new absorbance bands (prevailing the colorimetric method of detection).

The general mechanism involved in the turn-off detection of Hg2+ uses the interaction of fluorescent/metal nanoparticle probes and mercury ions to provide the quenching in the fluorescence under the interaction of analytes of interest [44–48]. With this aim, different fluorescent probes explored complexes such as imidazole/Hg2+ [48], pyrazoline as a fluorescent ion sensor [45], cysteine, and nitrogen-doped carbon dots by electron transfer from CDs to Hg2+ ions [49], and by the incorporation of sulfhydryl functional groups onto carbon dots. Yao et al. [50] reported a one-pot step process for the conversion of carbon dots using citric acid and 2,3,-dimercaptosuccinic as sources that resulted in a sulfhydryl functionalized carbon structure that favors the chelation provided by –SH groups in Hg2+ ions with the following quenching in the fluorescence of the CDs. In the absence of target molecules, an initial blue emission with a quantum yield of 15.8 % is converted to a wholly quenched emission under the presence of Hg2+ ions. A general scheme for the turn-off process under the detection of Hg2+ is depicted in Fig. 3 , which summarizes the association of compounds containing carbon dots (blue circles) modified by –SH, - COOH, and –NH2 groups incorporated onto CD's surface. The thiophilic behavior of the mercury ions favors the inhibition of fluorescence of the CDs due to the binding of Hg2+ ions into the CD surface.

Similarly, N-doped carbon dots prepared by a reaction between citron fruit (carbon source) and human urine (nitrogen source) use the electron transfer process between N-doped carbons and Hg2+, which is responsible for the turn-off in the fluorescence [49]. Corresponding CDs produced with citric acid and ethylenediamine use a static quenching process by recombination of non-radiative electrons and holes [51].

Developing design strategies for producing Hg2+ fluorescent probe molecules that activate their fluorescence in the presence of the contaminant must consider the probes' solubility in water, the detection time, and the combined sensitivity/selectivity of the compound. Intrinsic advantages of turn-on probes are the minimal effects of false positives (observed in turn-off-based sensors), reduced environmental interference, and adequate operating conditions [16,52,53]. The general process for probe development is based on the coordination bond and chemical bond formation (such as Hg–O, Hg–S, and Hg–C) with the thiophilicity behavior explored for the interaction with gold nanoparticles, improving the selectivity for the specific probe molecule [17]. Organic dyes such as rhodamine, coumarin, and fluorescein are relevant active components for detecting analytes, with many papers exploring rhodamine derivatives [54–57].

Rhodamine is a group of xanthene-derivative molecules [58,59] characterized by high fluorescent quantum yield, longer excitation, and emission wavelength [60,61]. In particular, rhodamine B, rhodamine 6G, and derivatives have been considered in several applications (since active fluorescent probes for random lasers [62,63] until colorimetric and fluorescent probes to sense different cations [64,65]).

The general mechanism for detecting cations from rhodamine derivatives is based on the opening of the spirolactam ring under complexation with cation species. The spirocyclic form provides negligible fluorescence and no color to the rhodamine derivatives solution. Under the presence of cationic species, the spirocyclic form is converted to an open cyclic structure, with the spirolactam ring opening resulting in a change in color and providing the characteristic fluorescence of rhodamine [60,61,66,67].

The detection of analytes is favored by the rhodamine's high solubility in water and good biocompatibility [63], which favors its application as a biosensor, bioimaging agent for the identification of cancer cells [68], and fluorescent probe for stimulated emission depletion microscopy [69]. In addition, regarding detecting Hg2+ ions, rhodamine B-based chemosensors are critical for producing point-of-care tests with the simple detection of targeted contaminants (lab-at-home) [66].

Based on these properties, the rhodamine derivative is one of the most used probe molecules for turn-on detection of Hg2+. Modifying the rhodamine B lactam structure with 3-methyl-2-thiophene carboxaldehyde [22] uses the affinity of mercury (II) to thiol groups, resulting in mutual application as a colorimetric and turn-on sensor. The use of rhodamine spirolactam derivative makes use of the emission of the ring-opened structures (in the presence of Hg2+) [52] or by functionalization of rhodamine 6G with N-methyl imidazole unity [16].

Alternative strategies explore the strong fluorescence of rhodamine B in bulk solution against the weak fluorescence of fluorophore when disposed of as an adsorbed species. This process has been analyzed using pairs of rhodamine B and gold nanoparticles that result in quenching fluorescence due to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and collision of rhodamine B and AuNPs. Under the presence of cationic species (such as polymyxin B molecules [64] or Hg2+ ions [70]), rhodamine B molecules are released into the bulk of the solution, and a substantial increase in the fluorescence takes place, indicating the presence of cationic species dispersed into the solution. The requisites of selectivity of captured ions require an additional step of chemical modification of gold nanoparticles by incorporating thiol ligands.

The general mechanism for the process of selective detection of mercury (II) in water is shown in Fig. 4 . As can be seen in Fig. 4a, the initial configuration of aggregates of Au NPs/rhodamine B (non-fluorescent aggregate by the FRET established between components) is changed to a final configuration in which Hg2+ ions are reduced to Hg0 by citrate disposed of as a stabilizing component on gold nanoparticles and rhodamine B molecules that are released to the bulk solution, providing their typical fluorescence, indicative of the contaminant presence in solution. Modifications by thiol-based group incorporation and PDCA-thiol-based sensors are schematically drawn in Fig. 4b and c – these compounds favor the sensor's selectivity to Hg2+. As a result, outstanding detection for mercury (II) reached values of LOD as low as 2 ppb [70], confirming the potential for fluorescent (turn-on)-based methods of detection.

Different Hg2+ turn-on-based sensors have also been considered alternatives to the rhodamine-based sensors from aggregation-induced emission processes [23], coumarin-based probes containing perimidine moieties [68], Ferrier carbocyclization [71], and DNA-based fluorescent probes [72] by the thymine-Hg-thymine bonding, which explores that the folding of parts of the DNA activates the fluorescence of the quenched fluorophore [72].

Modifying optical fibers by incorporating a fluorescent probe is the standard method for providing sensing properties to the template fibers. The typical active agents are biomaterials (protein and DNA), luminescent nanostructures (semiconductor quantum dots and carbon dots) [73], gold nanoparticles [74] and nanorods [75], and silver nanoparticles [76].

The advantages of the change in the refractive index of fiber sensors are facilitated by the surface modification of the compounds, such as Al2O3 nanoparticles sensitized by 4-(2-pyridyl azo)-resorcinol compounds [77], and poly acrylic acid (PAA) coated gold nanoparticles that explore their affinity to metallic mercury to form amalgam alloy [74]. On the other hand, quantum dots [28] explore the evanescence wave sensing provoked by the turn-off process that results from the interaction of QCDs and Hg2+ ions optimized by covalent bonding of QCDs and the surface of the optical fiber.

Ruan et al. [78] reported the development of coating layers for the end face of optical fibers composed of silica sol-gel doped with Rhod-5N (a complex of 5N-BAPTA ionophore and rhodamine), exploring the change in fluorescence as a detection mechanism.

Photonic crystals (PC) have been considered a promising backbone for the optical detection of Hg2+, exploring the chemical immobilization of compounds for improvement in the fluorescent detection but also the colorimetric and semiquantitative detection by naked eyes, making use of several prototypes such as carbon quantum dots, metal nanoclusters, small organic molecules, peptides, and DNA [79,80].

As a standard application for photonic crystals, the red shift and the blue shift in the diffraction pattern have been explored using polymer crystalline colloidal arrays that change their hydrogel volume under interaction with analytes [81] (blue-shifted diffraction) or by using nanoporous silicon applied in Bragg reflectors [82].

On the other hand, the enhancement in fluorescence is observed in carbon dots immobilized on polystyrene spheres as a middle layer in a double heterostructure colloidal photonic crystal [83] with a limit of detection of 91 pM [83]. Alternatively, structures based on inverse opal photonic crystals with porphyrin-infiltrated SiO2 explored the compound's characteristic fluorescence enhancement under interaction with Hg2+ [84]. Another application for inverse opal polymeric photonic crystals explores the inhibition in the urease hydrolysis change provoked by Hg2+ ions, resulting in a red shift in the Bragg diffraction net, making the naked eye detection of the color change in the PC [85] (colorimetric-based method of detection).

A significant advantage of photonic crystals (applied as a backbone for different experimental systems) is the typical enhancement in the response to analyte traces. Liu et al. described a critical enhancement in the quantum yield of metal nanoclusters (8-fold for gold-silver nanocluster) due their incorporation in PC, allowing LOD for mercury detection to reach values of 0.35 nM [86] (fluorescence-based method for detection).

Carbon dots are excellent environment-friendly fluorescent prototypes obtained from the hydrothermal conversion of carbonaceous waste sources. These structures are characterized by a wide range of quantum yield (reaching values of 46.6 % [87]) and promising applications in bioimaging and biosensors for water-soluble compounds with good biocompatibility and low toxicity [88–90].

One of the most simple and efficient experimental setups is based on carbon dots as active molecules with tunable fluorescence applied to detect mercury (II) ions. For this purpose, surface functionalization/passivation is required to activate the turn-on process under specific binding with target species [12]. Correia et al. [91] explored the doping of carbon dots by Eu (III) in a strategy that favors the mutual fluorescence of components (red for Eu3+ ions and a broadband for CD centered at 450 nm). With the quenching in the luminescence for both compounds in the presence of mercury, the ratio in the fluorescence of Eu3+ and CD is explored to quantify the nature and the amount of detected contaminant. Black phosphorous quantum dots are also applied as fluorescent probes to detect Hg2+ [92].

To improve the detection limit, the heteroatom-doped carbon dots are particularly interesting for detecting specific analytes. In particular, Hg2+ ions are strongly sulfophilic, allowing that S atoms can be easily integrated with mercury (II) ions [93]. Consequently, the heteroatom doping with sulfhydryl groups represents an essential step in reaching the desirable specific detection of Hg2+ ions. With a typical turn-off detection mode [87,93–96] in the fluorescence, carbon dots have been converted from different natural sources such as China grass carp scales [93], Tamarindus indica leaves [87], citric acid/ethylenediamine [95], pigeon features [94], and considered for several applications. In particular, outstanding performance in Hg2+ detection was observed for N, S-co-doped fluorescent CDs (10.3 nM) [94], S-doped CD (14 nM) [93], and Tamarindus-based CD (6 nM) [87] by exploring fluorescent-based mechanisms of detection.

Electrospinning is a versatile electrostatic technique to produce polymeric fiber nets. The condition to obtain regular polymeric fibers depends on a critical balance involving several parameters, such as the viscosity, conductivity, and flux of the polymeric solution along the spinneret [97,98]. A basic experimental setup for producing fibers consists of infusion pumping that provides a controlled flux of polymerics solution and a spinneret disposed in front of a grounded target (static or dynamic collector). Under a high voltage (in the range of 5–20 kV), the droplet is elongated as a result of the competition established between the surface tension and the resulting electric force that separates charges on the droplet and initiates the formation of Taylor's cone with the ejection of the solution in direction to the target. During the flight, the solvent evaporates, and the resulting fiber nets are progressively deposited on the grounded target [99–103]. The incorporation of nanoparticles of interest into the polymeric solution (with minimal influence on the optimized parameters for fiber preparation) can result in regular fibers and uniform distribution of nanostructures along with the synthesized material, making possible the mutual (surface and bulk) modification of the fibrillar structure for areal and volumetric (by diffusive forces) interaction with analytes of interest.
Another important advantage explored in nanostructure-based systems is the electrospun fibers' high specific surface area for applications in optical sensing platforms [104].

Based on these several advantages of electrospun fibers (in particular for cost-effectiveness, high specific surface area, and smooth surface functionalization [105,106]), it has been explored electrospun fibers as muscular scaffolds [107], biosensors [104], and nanofibers-based optical sensors [108].

Sensing mechanisms by modified electrospun fibers explored a variety of responses, such as electrochemical detection [109] and aggregation-induced emission [110]. The most common method for the use of electrospun fibers in Hg2+ detection is the colorimetric and fluorescent-based methods that are favored by the intrinsic structure of nanofibers (highly porous nature and ease of functionalization, associated with high specific surface area [111]) making possible the use of naked eye detection of target molecules [112]. The specific binding of ions on the surface of nanofibers depends on the adequate functionalization of nanofibers [113] exploring sensing materials that react to the presence of ions with Stokes shift or providing a reduction/increase in the fluorescence of species with high quantum yield [114].

With this aim, standard metal nanoparticles and nanoclusters have been extensively explored as active sensing elements in flexible nanofibers applied as optical and electrochemical sensors. Romaguera-Barcelay et al. [109] explored the dispersion of AgNPs oxidized under contact with Hg2+ converted to Hg0 deposited on the surface of metal nanoparticles, forming Ag–Hg amalgam. Similarly, the effects of amalgam formation on fluorescence and colorimetric detection are measured using plasmon band resonance (changes in the absorption of light-based detection mechanisms). For these applications, the control in the disposition of metal nanoparticles on the surface of electrospun fibers have been considered as part of an effort to avoid the diffusion of Hg2+ ions along with the bulk of the electrospun fibers, enhancing the sensitivity of the overall sensing system. Senthamizhan et al. [115] reported the chemical modification of fibers to anchor gold nanoclusters on the surface of the electrospun fiber, reaching a limit of detection of ∼50 ppt with typical advantages of the real-time monitoring approach (changes in SPR behavior detection mechanism). Images of scanning electron microscopy shown in Fig. 5 a to c confirm the formation of electrospun fibers in the presence of gold nanoclusters (Fig. 5a) and then the presence (b) and (c) absence of BSA ligands. Elemental mapping explored the surface of a single fiber and confirmed the presence of elements carbon and oxygen in the core of the fibers with gold and sulfur-based groups disposed of in their surfaces (see Fig. 5d). The emission of gold nanoclusters is shown in Fig. 5e for the net and individual fiber in Fig. 5f.

To illustrate the limit of detection of a single fiber in contact with an ultralow concentration of mercury (II) ions, the fluorescence of electrospun fibers is shown in Fig. 6 for blank, 1 ppt and 100 ppt (a) with the preserved morphology (b) and a clear reduction in the fluorescence (quenching in the presence of Hg2+ ions - (c)), confirming the outstanding performance for the corresponding electrospun-based sensing system.

In addition, promising active fluorescent compounds for Hg2+ ions detection have been considered in carbon quantum dots-loaded electrospun fibers as active turn-off elements in association with magnetite nanoparticles (Fe3O4) as a component applied in the magnetic removal of electrospun fibers from the reactor [116]. Alternatively to the standard method of preparation of fibers by electrospinning, this process is favored by the following use as an adsorbent/sensor of Hg2+ with the quenching in the fluorescence after 150 min of contact with the analyte. The additional magnetic separation step is critical for removing the adsorbed species with the regeneration and recycling conditions for several uses of the electrospun fiber-based sensors.

Similarly, Wang et al. [117] reported the covalent binding of poly(acrylic acid) and poly(pyrene methanol), which are also affected by the quenching fluorescence under the presence of Hg2+. As observed, one of the most important advantages of encapsulated species into electrospun fibers is the possibility of control in the localization of active molecules on the fiber surface that results in increasing sensitivity, which is associated with the possibility of the removal by adsorption of Hg2+ ions from water by filtering of fibers, making use of the magnetic separation, introducing this removal step as an additional advantage for metal detection.
For comparison, Table 1 summarizes the detection limit for different experimental systems prepared by active molecules (sensing elements) exploring different detection methods.

A wide range of variations in the LOD are observed with typical values in order of nM for photonic crystals and colorimetric-based systems. Relative to the emissive-based sensors, a lower detection limit is observed for gold nanoclusters and rhodamine-based systems that reached values in order of pM and outstanding performance for electrospun decorated with gold nanoclusters with LOD of 50 ppt, confirming the potential of nanofibers-based sensing elements.

The incorporation of photoluminescent probe molecules (dyes and metal nanoclusters) into fibrillar structures has been accomplished by advantages in the lowest values for the limit of detection of mercury (II) ions reaching outstanding values below the acceptable limit established by WHO for traces of contaminant in drinking water. Requisites of selectivity have been successfully conducted by the surface functionalization of fibers′ surfaces favored by the high specific surface area, the specific binding with thiol-based groups, and the formation of amalgam bonding. The big challenge regards reducing the LOD for colorimetric (naked eye detection), given the point-of-care applications with real-time detection in low-cost prototypes that require minimal instrumentation for operation (avoiding, for example, the use of complex emission experimental setup). In addition to the simple identification of traces of mercury (II) ions, the adsorption of ions, regeneration, and reuse of devices represent relevant requisites that are associated with the concept of green chemistry and environmental preservation, allowing the identification of contaminants in remote regions, such as the Yanomami Indigenous Villages (Brazilian Amazon) severely affected by contamination with mercury (II) ions.
